Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd kind of drift through the week and then realize I had stuff due soon. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I felt like it or when something caught my attention. I'm a visual learner, so I'd watch videos or look at diagrams when I could, but I wouldn't say I had any organized schedule. It just happened when it happened.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I definitely wasn't one of those students who sits down at the same time every day. Deadlines would sometimes push me to cram, but other times I'd just let things slide if I wasn't feeling it. My extracurricular activities took up time too, so studying fit around whatever else was going on in my life.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I remembered there was something due or when I got a notification. Sometimes I'd just be bored and scroll through to see if anything looked interesting. It wasn't really a conscious decision most of the time—more like I'd think 'oh right, that class exists' and then check in. The videos were usually what pulled me back.
Q4: Not really a routine, no. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in more regularly because the content was actually engaging or I had some momentum going. But it never stuck as a habit. I'd get distracted by other things or just lose interest, and then I'd be back to logging in sporadically.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me. I'm visual, so watching someone explain things with diagrams or demonstrations made way more sense than reading dense text. The discussion boards were okay too when people actually had interesting takes. I'd sometimes get ideas from peer interactions that helped me understand stuff better.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. They felt like a lot of effort for not much payoff. If something looked like it was going to take forever to get through, I'd skip it or just skim the highlights. I also didn't bother with some of the optional quizzes because they didn't count for anything.
Q7: If I could see the point of it right away, I'd do it. Like if a video clearly explained something I needed for an assignment, that felt worth my time. But if something seemed like busywork or I couldn't connect it to anything concrete, I'd lose interest fast. I need to see the relevance or I just drift off.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like it didn't matter. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, why bother? Required activities at least had some urgency to them. I know that's probably not the best attitude, but when motivation is low, you prioritize what you have to do over what you could do.
Q9: Definitely. Once I downloaded lecture slides or videos, I'd study from those on my phone or laptop without being connected to the LMS. I'd also look things up on YouTube if the course videos didn't click for me. Sometimes I'd take photos of diagrams and review them later. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded materials or working through problems on paper that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd rewatch videos I'd already downloaded or go over notes I'd made. The LMS only sees when you're clicking around on it, not when you're actually thinking about the content.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it or screenshot the key parts, and then go back to those files whenever I needed to review. It was easier than logging back in every time. So my LMS activity looked minimal even though I was still using the materials.
Q12: There were stretches where I just couldn't be bothered. Nothing dramatic was happening—I just felt disconnected from the whole thing. My motivation has always been kind of low, so when the initial interest wore off, it got harder to push myself. The CBT sessions I did helped a bit with my mood, but they didn't magically make me want to study more.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. With low resources, I'm often juggling stuff like part-time work and other responsibilities. When I'm tired, the LMS is the last thing I want to deal with. Confusion sometimes made me avoid things too—if I didn't get something, I'd put it off instead of asking for help.
Q14: Oh for sure. Sometimes I'd be logged in, clicking through stuff, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything. It looked like engagement on the surface, but I was just going through the motions.
Q15: All the time. I'd know something was important but still put it off because I wasn't in the right headspace. Low motivation makes it hard to start things even when you know you should. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then later never really came until the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me moving. I'd see something was due soon and suddenly find the energy to engage. Without that pressure, I'd drift. It's not ideal, but the deadline created urgency that my own motivation couldn't.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it. Seeing that I did well on something gave me a little boost, made me feel like maybe I wasn't completely lost. But if feedback was delayed or generic, it didn't really change anything for me. I needed that immediate connection to feel like my effort mattered.
Q18: The structure was okay, I guess. Having things laid out in modules helped me know what existed, even if I didn't engage with all of it. But the flexibility also meant I could easily skip things without immediate consequences. A tighter structure might have kept me more on track, honestly.
Q19: The video content being front and center made engagement easier since that's how I learn best. But having so many optional resources made it easy to ignore stuff. Also, when things weren't clearly connected to assessments, I'd deprioritize them. Clearer pathways would've helped.
Q20: I think sometimes people are exploring or trying to figure out what they need to know without actually producing work. Or they might be struggling and clicking around looking for help but not finding it. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes you're just lost in the system.
Q21: That's basically me. My attendance was low and I didn't complete everything, but I still did well on exams and assignments when I actually did them. I learn efficiently when I focus, and I use outside resources a lot. The LMS activity doesn't capture that I'm actually pretty good at picking up concepts quickly.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you were technically on a page. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or if you understood anything. They miss offline study, they miss thinking, they miss all the learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and thinking about it beyond just completing tasks. It's not about how many times you log in—it's about whether the material is actually sticking and whether you're curious about it. I wasn't always engaged by that definition, but when I was, it showed in my work.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm actually pretty happy most of the time despite my low motivation. It wouldn't show the offline studying I do or the fact that I can perform well when I actually try. It also wouldn't capture that my disengagement isn't about the course being bad—it's more about my own internal struggles with motivation and energy.
